name: Bevy
description: Modern open-source Rust game engine built around ECS architecture. High performance and strong systems-level control for teams comfortable with Rust.

name: Cocos Creator
description: Open-source engine focused on 2D and lightweight 3D with a component workflow and TypeScript scripting. Popular for mobile and web games, especially in Asia.

name: Construct 3
description: Browser-based no-code/low-code 2D game engine with event-sheet scripting. Fast onboarding for non-programmers and educators with web-first deployment.

name: Defold
description: Lightweight free engine with Lua scripting and strong 2D capabilities. Uses a component-based architecture and supports native, web, and mobile exports.

name: GameMaker
description: Specialized 2D game engine with GML scripting. Used to make classics like Undertale and Hotline Miami. Fast prototyping, beginner-friendly.

name: Godot
description: Free and open-source game engine with GDScript (Python-like) and C# support. Lightweight, excellent for 2D games, rapidly improving 3D pipeline.

name: LOVE
description: Minimal open-source 2D framework using Lua. Code-first approach with a very fast feedback loop and excellent fit for prototyping and game jams.

name: Phaser
description: HTML5 game framework for browser-based 2D games using JavaScript/TypeScript. Excellent for web-native games and game jams.
cons: JavaScript can be slow vs native

name: Unity
description: The world's most popular cross-platform game engine, used for 2D, 3D, VR, and AR games. C# scripting, massive asset store, excellent beginner support.

name: Unreal Engine
description: AAA-quality 3D game engine by Epic Games. Industry standard for realistic graphics, large-scale games, and cinematics. Blueprints + C++ workflow.
pros: Photorealistic graphics out of the box

name: IntelliJ IDEA
description: JetBrains IDE focused on JVM and polyglot development with top-tier navigation, inspections, and plugin extensibility.
bestUseCase: Tooling, backend, and pipeline development for game projects that use JVM ecosystems.
cons: Can feel heavy for small scripts
Paid Ultimate features for advanced use

name: JetBrains Rider
description: Cross-platform .NET and C# IDE with strong refactoring, Unity support, and code analysis designed for professional workflows.
bestUseCase: Unity and .NET teams that want fast navigation, static analysis, and robust refactoring.
cons: Higher memory usage than lightweight editors

name: Sublime Text
description: Fast, lightweight text editor with extensibility and multi-cursor editing suited to scripting and quick code changes.
bestUseCase: Rapid editing for scripts, config files, and lightweight game tooling workflows.
cons: Not a full IDE out of the box

name: Visual Studio
description: Full-featured Microsoft IDE with deep C# and C++ tooling, advanced debugging, profiling, and native Unity workflows.
bestUseCase: Large C# or C++ game codebases on Windows with strong debugging requirements.

name: Visual Studio Code
description: Free, extensible code editor with first-class TypeScript and C# support, debugging, and a deep extension marketplace.
bestUseCase: General-purpose IDE for game logic, scripts, and tooling across all engines.

name: Diversion
description: Game-focused source control aimed at artist-friendly binary workflows and simpler collaboration UX than traditional Git setups.

name: Git + GitHub
description: The standard version control system for code. GitHub provides hosting, pull requests, and CI/CD integrations.
cons: Poor at handling large binary files (art, audio)
Need Git LFS for binaries (extra cost)
Command line can be daunting for beginners

name: GitLab
description: Git hosting platform with integrated CI/CD, issue tracking, and self-hosting options. Useful for teams needing all-in-one DevOps workflows.
cons: Can be heavy to administer self-hosted

name: Perforce Helix Core
description: Enterprise version control system widely used in AAA game studios for very large binary assets and multi-site collaboration.

name: Plastic SCM (Unity DevOps)
description: Version control designed for game development with large binary file support. Now integrated into Unity DevOps.

name: Adobe Photoshop
description: Industry-standard raster image editor. Used for texture creation, UI assets, concept art, and post-processing.

name: After Effects
description: Adobe's motion graphics and compositing tool. Used for cinematic cutscene VFX and UI animations. Not real-time but exports to sprite sheets.
cons: Requires export to use in engine

name: Aseprite
description: The industry-standard pixel art and animation tool for game developers. Tilemaps, spritesheet export, frame-by-frame animation.
cons: Pixel art only (no vector or raster painting)

name: Autodesk Maya
description: Comprehensive DCC suite for 3D modeling, rigging, and pipeline integration. Widely used in AAA and studio production environments.

name: Blender
description: Free open-source 3D modeling, rigging, animation, and rendering suite. Used by indie studios worldwide for game assets.
cons: Can be slow for very large assets

name: Cascadeur
description: 3D keyframe animation software with physics-assisted posing and auto-physics features. Useful for more natural character motion without full mocap sessions.
cons: Narrow focus vs full DCC tools

name: DragonBones
description: Free 2D skeletal animation tool similar to Spine. Good for mobile game character animation with engine integrations.

name: EmberGen
description: Real-time volumetric fluid and smoke simulation tool used to author explosions, fire, and clouds. Exports baked assets for engine use.

name: Godot Control Nodes
description: Godot's built-in UI system using Control nodes in the scene tree. Straightforward, integrated with everything Godot.
cons: Less powerful than web-based UI solutions

name: Godot Particles
description: Godot's built-in particle system for 2D and 3D effects. CPU and GPU modes available, integrated with Godot's scene system.
cons: Less powerful than Unity VFX Graph

name: Houdini
description: Procedural 3D and VFX suite used for advanced simulations, destruction, and technical art pipelines. Houdini Engine enables integration into game editors.

name: Krita
description: Free open-source digital painting application. Great for concept art, textures, and 2D game art with a professional brush engine.
pros: Good for concept art and textures

name: Live2D Cubism
description: 2D mesh deformation and rigging system used for anime-style character motion. Common in visual novels, mobile RPGs, and VTuber-adjacent workflows.

name: Mixamo
description: Adobe service for automatic character rigging and a large library of mocap animations. Speeds up prototyping and placeholder animation work.

name: Niagara (UE5)
description: Unreal Engine 5's advanced node-based VFX framework for particles, simulations, and gameplay-reactive effects. Built for high-fidelity real-time visuals.

name: NoesisGUI
description: XAML-based retained-mode UI middleware for games with strong runtime performance and tooling. Used when teams want scalable app-like UI patterns in games.

name: Procreate
description: iPad-first digital painting app popular for concept sketches, UI mockups, and stylized 2D art ideation. Fast and intuitive for portable workflows.
pros: Great for ideation on the go

name: Pyxel Edit
description: Tile-focused pixel art editor built for fast environment and tileset creation. Popular in retro and top-down indie projects.

name: Rive
description: Real-time interactive animation and UI tool for runtime motion graphics. Useful for polished menus, onboarding flows, and animated HUD elements.

name: Spine
description: Professional 2D skeletal animation tool widely used in game development. Runtime available for Unity, Godot, and more.
pros: Smooth animations with small file sizes

name: Substance Designer
description: Node-based procedural material authoring tool for creating reusable PBR materials. Widely used in production pipelines for scalable asset generation.

name: Substance Painter
description: Industry-standard texture painting suite for PBR materials on 3D assets. Offers real-time viewport feedback and exports tuned for major engines.

name: Unity UI Toolkit
description: Unity's modern UI system using UXML/USS (similar to HTML/CSS). Best for complex, scalable game UI and tooling.
pros: Editor tool UI built with it too
cons: Learning curve for traditional game devs

name: Unity VFX Graph
description: Node-based visual effects editor for Unity's High Definition and Universal Render Pipelines. GPU-accelerated particle systems.

name: ZBrush
description: Digital sculpting package used for high-detail character and creature creation. Commonly used to produce high-poly sculpts for bake-down game assets.

name: Audacity
description: Free open-source audio editor for waveform editing, cleanup, and quick mastering tasks. Useful for indie teams handling simple sound effects and voice prep.

name: BFXR
description: Retro sound effect generator focused on quick arcade-style bleeps, impacts, and UI cues. Commonly used in game jams and pixel-art projects.

name: Eleven Labs TTS
description: Text-to-speech platform for generating narration and character voice placeholders with expressive output. Useful for iterating dialogue timing before final recording.
cons: Final polish may need manual editing

name: FMOD Studio
description: Professional interactive audio middleware used to author adaptive music and sound behavior in games. Integrates with major engines and supports parameter-driven mixing.

name: Reaper
description: Lightweight full-featured DAW widely used for game audio production and editing. Offers deep routing, scripting, and plugin support at a relatively low cost.
cons: UI less polished than some DAWs

name: Soundly
description: Cloud-connected sound effects library and browsing tool designed for fast search and drag-drop workflow. Helps teams source and organize production-ready SFX quickly.

name: Suno Music
description: AI music generation tool for quickly drafting soundtrack ideas and mood references. Useful in early production for testing pacing and tone before bespoke composition.
cons: Not a replacement for final composition in many projects

name: Wwise
description: Industry-standard game audio middleware for interactive music, voice, and effects with deep profiling tools. Common in mid-size and AAA production pipelines.

name: ChatGPT / GPT-4
description: General-purpose AI assistant by OpenAI. Useful for game design brainstorming, dialog writing, game mechanics analysis, and code debugging.

name: Claude Code
description: Agentic coding workflow centered on repository-aware planning and code edits. It is useful for larger refactors, debugging sessions, and implementation tasks that span multiple files.

name: Cursor
description: AI-first code editor built on VS Code with context-aware code generation and refactoring workflows. Useful for rapid scripting, codebase navigation, and implementation planning in game projects.
cons: Needs careful review of generated code

name: GitHub Copilot
description: AI pair programmer integrated into VS Code, Visual Studio, JetBrains IDEs. Autocompletes code, suggests implementations, writes tests.

name: Windsurf
description: AI coding environment with integrated chat and command-style editing workflows. It helps teams iterate on gameplay logic, utility scripts, and project tooling quickly.

name: Apple App Store
description: iOS and macOS app marketplace. Required for commercial iOS game distribution. Strict review process.

name: CrazyGames
description: Web game platform for instant-play HTML5 games with built-in distribution and monetization opportunities. Strong for rapid browser game launches.

name: Epic Games Store
description: PC game storefront by Epic with favorable revenue share terms and growing audience. Includes developer portal tooling and store integrations.

name: GOG Connect / GOG Galaxy
description: GOG distribution ecosystem focused on DRM-free PC releases and community trust. GOG Galaxy provides launcher and user account integration.

name: Google Play Store
description: Android's primary app and game marketplace. Required for commercial Android game distribution.

name: itch.io
description: Open indie game marketplace. Zero upfront cost, flexible revenue sharing, excellent for game jams and indie releases.

name: Poki
description: Web game publishing platform with a focus on polished, quick-loading browser experiences. Offers access to a broad casual audience.
cons: Limited fit for deep premium games

name: Steam
description: The dominant PC gaming platform by Valve. Required for most commercial PC game releases. Steamworks SDK for DRM, achievements, leaderboards.

name: Xbox / PlayStation / Nintendo Partner Portals
description: Official console publishing portals and certification pipelines for Xbox, PlayStation, and Nintendo platforms. Required path for commercial console releases.
pros: Access to platform SDK and certification docs
